intentional_test_features: Highly specific trigger: syncope precipitated immediately by a loud acoustic startle (alarm) is a hallmark red flag for Long QT syndrome (especially LQT2) - the AI Doctor must recognise this bizarre-but-classic trigger

Convulsive syncope mistaken for seizure: brief hypoxic stiffening from cerebral hypoperfusion, with rapid full recovery and no tongue-biting/post-ictal state, favours an arrhythmic (cardiac) cause over epilepsy
Family history of young sudden death (maternal aunt died at 25) is a pivotal red flag that must be actively elicited and heavily weighted
Structurally normal heart with a normal resting exam does NOT exclude an inherited channelopathy - diagnosis requires a 12-lead ECG (prolonged QTc) and specialist assessment
Ongoing trigger and recurrence risk: the acoustic trigger is still present in the environment and re-stimulation risks fatal Torsades de Pointes - immediate low-stimulus escalation is required

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-03)
